steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Byte Range Semantics · Theory Byte Range Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 8080 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to validate inclusive HTTP byte ranges and TypeScript stream cleanup; bound bytes, time, attempts, promises, streams, concurrency, cache, and spend; then deliver a duplicate or reordered event and prevent duplicate stakeholder effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, parse and evaluate single byte ranges, suffix ranges, satisfiable bounds, 206, Content-Range, Accept-Ranges, and 416.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Byte Range Semantics model · Theory Byte Range Semantics, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: A Range header requests a byte count rather than inclusive byte positions. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
